data.title: Airbus is said to weigh bigger A220 variant as early sales talks begin
data.description:
Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) is preparing to approach airlines and aircraft lessors about a larger version of its A220 regional jet, Reuters reported Thursday, citing industry sources, with the aim of moving toward a formal development launch later this year.

The effort centers on securing enough preliminary commitments to support a stretched A220-500 model that could seat around 180 passengers. The outreach marks the beginning of more substantive commercial discussions ahead of a possible launch decision, which could come as soon as the Farnborough Airshow in July.

Executives from Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) told financiers during the Airlines Economics conference in Dublin that 2026 is expected to be a pivotal year for the A220 program, adding that additional details would be shared in the near future.

Any move to proceed with development of the proposed aircraft, described by industry observers as a relatively straightforward stretch of the existing design, would still require approval from the Airbus (EADSF) (EADSY) board, Reuters reported.

data.title: From diapers to Tylenol: The household products mega-merger that will serve half the world's population
data.description:
Shareholders with Kimberly-Clark (KMB) and Kenvue (KVUE) approved the merger of the two companies on Thursday by an overwhelming margin. The transaction is expected to close in the second half of 2026, subject to the receipt of regulatory approvals and satisfaction of other customary closing conditions.

The merger brings together two major global players in consumer health and personal care to form a projected $32B revenue health and wellness leader anchored by a portfolio of complementary brands. Under the terms of the deal, Kimberly-Clark (KMB) will acquire all outstanding shares of Kenvue in a cash-and-stock transaction valuing Kenvue at about $48.7B, with Kenvue (KVUE) shareholders receiving $3.50 in cash plus 0.14625 Kimberly-Clark (KMB) shares per Kenvue share.

Upon closing, Kimberly-Clark (KMB) shareholders are expected to own approximately 54% of the combined company and Kenvue (KVUE) shareholders about 46%.

Strategically, the deal combines Kimberly-Clark's (KMB) tissue, baby, and personal care franchises with Kenvue's (KVUE) over-the-counter and consumer health brands, including Tylenol, Band-Aid, Listerine, Zyrtec, Neutrogena, and Aveeno, positioning the combined company to serve nearly half of the global population with a broadened lineup across health, hygiene, and skin care. Mike Hsu, Kimberly-Clark's (KMB) current chairman and CEO, will lead the combined company, with three Kenvue (KVUE) directors joining the Kimberly-Clark (KMB) board.

Morgan Stanley's assessment of the deal was that Kimberly-Clark (KMB) struck the deal at an attractive price for a solid portfolio of well-known brands. The key issue may be how quickly Kenvue's (KVUE) struggling brands can be rehabilitated.

ETFs with the largest weighting on KMB and KVUE combined are the iShares MSCI Global Sustainable Development Goals ETF (SGD), Vanguard Consumer Staples ETF (VDC), VanEck Morningstar Wide Moat Value ETF (MVAL), and the State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLP).

data.title: ManpowerGroup outlines Q1 2026 revenue growth forecast with margin expansion focus amid AI-driven transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ManpowerGroup Inc. (MAN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jonas Prising, Chairman & CEO, stated, "We're pleased with our fourth quarter results, which marked a clear shift to stabilization, led by enterprise demand and supported by disciplined execution and a continued commitment to cost optimization." He highlighted reported revenues of $4.7 billion with organic constant currency growth of 2%, and system-wide revenue of $5.1 billion. Prising emphasized sequential improvement through year-end and noted, "Clients remain deliberate in their hiring given the macro backdrop, yet engagement levels are steady and activity is becoming more consistent." He pointed to resilience in key markets such as France and outperformance in Italy, with the Manpower brand growing for three consecutive quarters in the U.S. and Experis showing narrowed declines.
  • Prising explained that cost discipline remains a core focus, citing a 4% constant currency reduction in SG&A during the quarter and the first positive operating profit in five quarters for Northern Europe. He also highlighted scaling of AI recruiter toolkits, resulting in a 7% increase in placement rates, and expansion of agentic AI coding assistants in the U.S.
  • Becky Frankiewicz, President & Chief Strategy Officer, detailed that the company is focused on "driving commercial excellence, evolving our core capabilities to better serve the business and infusing AI in the organization for today and tomorrow." She emphasized client demand for flexibility and advisory around AI's impact on workforce composition, signaling strategic realignment to meet evolving client and candidate needs.
  • John McGinnis, Executive VP, CFO & Head of Investor Relations, stated, "In the fourth quarter, we delivered reported revenues of $4.7 billion. System-wide revenue was $5.1 billion. Our fourth quarter revenue results represented organic constant currency growth of 2%." He noted, "Gross profit margin came in just below our guidance range, driven by lower permanent recruitment in Europe, while staffing margin came in as expected and consistent with the previous quarter year-over-year trend."

Outlook

  • The company is forecasting earnings per share for the first quarter of 2026 to be in the range of $0.45 to $0.55. McGinnis stated, "Our constant currency revenue guidance range is between a 1% decrease and a 3% increase and at the midpoint is a 1% increase." EBITA margin for the first quarter is projected to be up 10 basis points at the midpoint compared to the prior year. The estimated effective tax rate for the first quarter will be 43%.
  • Prising indicated, "Assuming current trends continue and, as we anniversary the tariff-related headwinds, we believe 2026 has the potential to represent an important inflection point for the business with a path towards sustainable organic growth and margin expansion."

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter reported revenues were $4.7 billion, with system-wide revenue at $5.1 billion and organic constant currency growth of 2%. Adjusted EBITA was $100 million with a 2.1% margin. Adjusted EPS was $0.92. SG&A expense was $686 million, with a 4% constant currency reduction.
  • The Manpower brand achieved 5% growth in the quarter. Experis declined by 6%, and Talent Solutions declined by 4%, both reflecting sequential improvement. Gross margin was 16.3%. Manpower comprised 62% of gross profit, Experis 22%, and Talent Solutions 16%. Free cash flow for the fourth quarter was $168 million, and net debt at quarter end was $806 million.

Q&A

  • Mark Marcon, Robert W. Baird: Asked about margin aspirations given AI and PowerSuite investments. McGinnis replied, "Even in a modest recovery scenario, you should expect EBITA margin improvement year-over-year over the next 4 years...we're very optimistic we have an opportunity to expand margin meaningfully...to get back to the 4.5% to 5% over time."
  • Andrew Steinerman, JPMorgan: Inquired about sustainable organic revenue growth and flexible workforce trends. Prising responded, "We're encouraged by the inflection points...in France and in the U.S. particularly. It's too early for us to call a broad-based recovery is in motion."
  • Kartik Mehta, Northcoast Research: Questioned the impact of enterprise demand on margins and cash flow. McGinnis said, "Most of that [enterprise mix] has worked its way through...pricing remains very rational...we have actions in place to continue to mitigate [DSO], and we expect ongoing progress in that in 2026."
  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: Asked for revenue trends by geography. McGinnis provided monthly sequential improvement figures, highlighting ongoing progress in France, stability in the U.S., and continued strength in Italy.
  • Joshua Chan, UBS: Sought details on SG&A leverage and gross margin stabilization. McGinnis explained, "We've done a lot of work...SG&A coming down 4% in constant currency in the fourth quarter...on GP margin...perm is lower and perm will come back in the future, but it's at historically low levels."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ sentiment was cautiously optimistic but pressed for clarity on margin recovery, market inflection, and the durability of enterprise-led stabilization. Questions often focused on the pace of recovery, mix impacts, and the sustainability of cost actions.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational rigor and strategic realignment, but was measured about the timing and breadth of recovery. In Q&A, management reiterated commitment to margin expansion and stabilization: "We are very encouraged by the results that we're seeing so far."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management reflected a slightly more positive outlook, driven by emerging signs of inflection and successful cost containment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a focus on stabilization in Q3 to explicit references to inflection and sequential improvement in Q4. Management cited specific growth in key geographies and a clearer path toward organic growth and margin expansion.
  • Strategic focus evolved from deploying foundational AI and digital tools to scaling these technologies for measurable commercial impact and productivity gains.
  • Analysts continued to probe margin durability, revenue mix, and recovery catalysts, but with increased attention to AI’s role and cost leverage in supporting future growth.
  • Key metrics improved for Manpower and Italy, with more stable or improving trends in the U.S. and France, as opposed to broader caution in Q3. Management’s tone remained confident but was more assertive about medium-term margin targets.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified continued macro uncertainty, political and budget instability in France, and uneven recovery across markets. Prising noted, "While uncertainty exists, our focus remains on supporting clients and executing in this evolving environment."
  • Lower permanent recruitment activity in Europe and enterprise mix shifts were cited as ongoing pressures on gross margin.
  • Cash flow timing and DSO increases related to enterprise client mix were acknowledged, with mitigation strategies underway.

Final Takeaway

ManpowerGroup management underscored a return to sequential growth, stabilization across major markets, and a robust trajectory for margin expansion, all backed by structural cost actions and scaled digital transformation. While not yet calling a broad-based recovery, leadership expressed confidence in the company’s ability to leverage AI and operational discipline for sustainable organic growth and improved profitability into 2026, with continued vigilance on market risks and evolving client needs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Quantum seeking buyer for Turkish copper mine - Bloomberg
data.description:
First Quantum Minerals (FQVLF) is looking to sell its Cayeli copper and zinc mine in Turkey as it seeks to strengthen its balance sheet, Bloomberg reported late Wednesday.

First Quantum started a sales process this month for Cayeli, one of Turkey's largest underground mining operations but among the company's smaller assets, which is valued at ~$400M, according to the report.

The company has sought to offload assets since Panama's government ordered a shutdown of its flagship Cobre Panama copper mine in late 2023, cutting its cash flow.

The Cayeli operation, located on the Black Sea coast in northeast Turkey, produced 11,491 metric tons of copper in 2024.

data.title: Munters Group AB (publ) reports Q4 results
data.description:
• Munters Group AB (publ) press release (MMNNF): Q4 Earnings per share amounted to SEK -0.06 (0.85).
  • Net sales declined -8%, with growth in and FoodTech, while AirTech declined. Currency effects impacted sales by -8%.

data.title: Germany orders sale of pipeline operator as condition for Sunoco's TanQuil takeover
data.description:
Germany's government said Thursday it will require the sale of the unit operating a vital military fuel pipeline network as a condition of Sunoco LP's (SUN) takeover of TanQuid, the country's largest independent petrochemical and biofuel storage operator.

The government declined to elaborate on the decision, which was made as part of an investment review process meant to clamp down on security risks and echoes past interventions aimed at blocking foreign control - particularly by Chinese firms - of critical infrastructure.

The government already owns 51% of the TanQuid unit in question, and has asked for the defense ministry to acquire the rest as a condition for the deal, according to a government report seen by Bloomberg.

Sunoco's (SUN) purchase of TanQuid was approved and completed earlier this month; Macquarie Asset Management announced the sale in May 2025.

data.title: Allegro MicroSystems outlines 22% year-over-year Q4 sales growth target while advancing data center and robotics initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Allegro MicroSystems (ALGM) Q3 2026

Management View

  • President and CEO Michael Doogue stated, "We continue to see positive momentum across the business, once again achieving growth in bookings and backlog to multi-quarter highs and securing significant design wins in our strategic focus areas led by ADAS, xEV and Data Center." He emphasized strong third quarter results, reporting "sales above the high end of our guidance range at $229 million and EPS above the midpoint of our guidance range at $0.15."
  • Doogue highlighted that "E-Mobility led continued growth in third quarter automotive sales," driven by content gains and increased adoption of xEV and ADAS systems. He added, "This momentum is reflected in our third quarter automotive design wins where E-Mobility led the quarter, in ADAS, we secured key wins for position sensors and motor drivers and electronic power steering systems."
  • In the industrial sector, Doogue stated, "sales growth was again led by data center, establishing a new quarterly record at 10% of sales, up 31% sequentially." Allegro also reported new design wins in robotics, with customer engagements indicating opportunities for high content in advanced humanoid robots.
  • The company introduced a new current sensor IC that "cuts power-related losses by up to 90%, enabling new levels of power density in xEV and data center applications." Doogue also noted the release of Allegro's first isolated gate driver IC for silicon carbide transistors, broadening the company's portfolio in both automotive and industrial markets.
  • Executive VP and CFO Derek D'Antilio reported, "Net sales were $229 million and non-GAAP earnings per share were $0.15. As a percentage of sales, gross margin was 49.9%, operating margin was 15.4% and adjusted EBITDA was 20.1% of sales." D'Antilio stated, "Total Q3 sales increased by 7% sequentially and 29% year-over-year."

Outlook

  • D'Antilio provided guidance for Q4 2026: "We expect fourth quarter sales to be in the range of $230 million to $240 million. The midpoint of this range equates to a 22% year-over-year increase."
  • He projected gross margin to be between "49% and 51%" and operating expenses to increase by approximately 3% sequentially, primarily due to payroll tax resets.
  • Non-GAAP EPS is expected to be "between $0.14 and $0.18 per share."
  • D'Antilio stated, "Interest expense is projected to be $5 million in Q4, which includes approximately $700,000 of expenses related to this repricing."

Financial Results

  • Allegro reported "net income was $29 million or $0.15 per diluted share. EPS increased by 15% sequentially and 114% year-over-year on sales increases of 7% and 29%, demonstrating the significant operating leverage in our business model."
  • Cash ended at $163 million, with cash flow from operations at $45 million and free cash flow at $41 million, representing 18% of Q3 sales. DSO improved to 40 days and inventory days were 133.
  • Sales to automotive customers increased by 6% sequentially and 28% year-over-year, with E-Mobility sales up 46% year-over-year. Data center sales reached a new quarterly record, now 10% of total sales.

Q&A

  • Timothy Arcuri, UBS: Asked about gross margin dynamics. D'Antilio responded, "the gross margin was largely geographic and product mix... China was 30% of our sales in the third quarter."
  • Arcuri inquired about sell-in versus sell-through. D'Antilio confirmed, "POS and sell-in were close to each other. Sell-in was slightly higher than POS. Going forward, I'd expect those 2 to be about equal."
  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: Asked about automotive growth into March. D'Antilio stated, "it will absolutely be led by Industrial. I expect auto to be about flat to marginally down, again, led by Chinese New Year."
  • Quatrochi followed up on auto customer inventory. Doogue replied, "We have yet to see any meaningful increases in inventory at the Tier 1s in automotive... we see fairly lean inventory out there."
  • Blayne Curtis, Jefferies: Sought clarity on Data Center product pipeline. Doogue explained, "the biggest piece of the business today... continues to be the fan drivers. On the gate drivers, big opportunity there... We would expect to see revenue in that space start to ramp somewhere in the 18-month to 24-month time frame."
  • Curtis asked about Data Center mix impact on margins. D'Antilio said, "current sensors... have slightly better gross margins. So as we continue to move in that direction... the margin will continue to improve."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised several probing questions about margins, inventory trends, and segment outlooks, reflecting a slightly cautious but constructive tone.
  • Management's responses were confident and detailed, with Doogue and D'Antilio repeatedly emphasizing strategic wins, growth drivers, and operational leverage. Phrases such as "We feel good about what we're seeing in automotive, strong bookings and backlog, great design win activity" showed continued optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst questions remain focused but show more interest in near-term headwinds and the timing of new business ramps. Management's tone in Q3 was consistently confident and proactive, similar to the prior quarter.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q4 sales increased from previous quarter's Q3 range, with the midpoint moving from $220 million to $235 million.
  • Gross margin guidance range remained steady at 49%-51%.
  • Management highlighted stronger sequential growth in data center and industrial segments, with automotive expected to be flat to slightly down due to seasonal effects.
  • Analysts in Q3 continued to question gross margins and sales mix, but there was a greater focus on Data Center and robotics opportunities.
  • Management's confidence in operating leverage and new product ramps (e.g., current sensors, isolated gate drivers, robotics) was more pronounced in the current quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that gross margin was affected by "geographic and product mix" and that China accounted for 30% of sales, which can impact margins.
  • Seasonal effects such as Chinese New Year are expected to impact automotive sales in the next quarter.
  • No significant inventory build-up observed at Tier 1 auto customers, with inventories remaining lean.
  • Some variability in OpEx due to variable compensation and payroll tax resets, but management expects OpEx to grow at the rate of inflation after Q4.

Final Takeaway

Allegro MicroSystems management emphasized robust momentum in design wins and bookings, with continued growth in key markets such as data center, E-Mobility, and robotics. The company expects strong year-over-year sales growth in Q4, bolstered by innovation in current sensing and gate driver technologies. Management continues to see significant operating leverage, disciplined OpEx management, and high confidence in its strategic direction, even as near-term automotive growth is tempered by seasonal factors.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Washington Trust outlines 5% loan growth target and margin expansion through new commercial hires
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Washington Trust Bancorp, Inc. (WASH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Edward Handy reported "continued earnings momentum and improving profitability" for the quarter, driven by "margin expansion, continued in-market deposit growth and increased revenues from wealth management." Handy noted targeted investments in Wealth Management and Commercial Banking, including the acquisition of assets from Lighthouse Financial Management and the hiring of a new Chief Commercial Banking Officer, Jim Brown. Handy announced the onboarding of an institutional banking team focused on education, health care, and nonprofit sectors in the Northeast, stating this would bring "high-quality C&I loans and strong deposit opportunities." He also highlighted a planned de novo branch opening in Pawtucket, Rhode Island, for later in 2026 to expand presence in a fast-growing area.
  • CFO Ronald Ohsberg stated, "In the fourth quarter, we reported net income of $16 million or $0.83 per share compared to $10.8 million or $0.56 per share for the preceding quarter." Ohsberg added, "Net interest income was $40.7 million, up by 5% from Q3 and 24% year-over-year. The margin was 2.56%, up by 16 basis points and up by 61 basis points year-over-year. A better funding mix with higher in-market deposits and lower wholesale funding as well as deposit rate management contributed to this improvement."

Outlook

  • CFO Ohsberg projected that, following a swap termination at the end of April, the margin is expected to "increase 9 basis points related to that item and another 4 basis points in the third quarter," leading to a "run rate benefit of 13 basis points that will be fully baked in, in the third quarter." He further projected "organic expansion" of "3 to 4 basis points per quarter," resulting in a Q4 estimate for 2026 of "2.78% to 2.82%."
  • Ohsberg expects "4% to 5% growth in CRE" for the year and indicated, "The C&I team, we think, will grow at a rate faster than that," with total loan growth targeted at "a very solid 5% year-over-year, which is an improvement over where we've been in 2025."
  • Noninterest expense in Q1 2026 is expected to increase by 6%, with a year-over-year 5% increase for all other expenses. A new branch will add approximately $600,000 to the expense run rate for the year.

Financial Results

  • Net income for Q4 was $16 million or $0.83 per share. Net interest income reached $40.7 million. The net interest margin was 2.56%. Noninterest income was up 5% compared to Q3, with wealth management revenues up 5% and average AUA for the fourth quarter increasing by 4%.
  • Mortgage banking revenues totaled $3.3 million, with origination and sales volumes up by 21% and 25%, respectively. The mortgage pipeline at year-end was $81 million. Full year mortgage originations were $667 million.
  • Noninterest expense for Q4 was $38 million, with salaries and benefits up by $973,000 or 4%. Other noninterest expenses included a $1 million charitable foundation contribution. Total equity was $544 million, up by $11 million from Q3.
  • The dividend remained at $0.56 per share.
  • Provision for credit losses normalized, and asset quality metrics improved, with nonaccruing loans at 25 basis points on total loans. There were net recoveries of $160,000 in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Mark Fitzgibbon, Piper Sandler: Asked about the sustainability of the mid-2.50% margin and future margin outlook. Ohsberg responded with detailed margin guidance, including the impact of a swap termination and expectations for organic expansion.
  • Fitzgibbon questioned the reserve coverage level. Chief Risk Officer William Wray explained, "We are on the lower side of the spectrum with our peers, although not unduly so... we're comfortable in that mid-70 coverage range just based on our portfolio and the loss estimates for it."
  • Fitzgibbon inquired about wealth management M&A. Handy clarified, "I was referring specifically to the institutional banking team... focused on endowments and retirement funds."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Sought details on expense outlook and variability. Ohsberg provided a breakdown of expected increases and the impact of the new branch and institutional team.
  • Del Monte asked about loan growth expectations. Ohsberg projected 5% overall loan growth, with specifics for CRE and C&I. Handy added context on credit formation and payoffs, noting high expectations for the new C&I team.
  • Laura Hunsicker, Seaport: Asked about the size and background of the new C&I team, their geographic focus, expense line details, and the outlook for charitable foundation contributions. Handy and Ohsberg addressed team composition, focus, and planned expense levels.
  • Hunsicker inquired about branch expansion and credit quality, including specifics on a classified office loan. Wray described the loan as "extremely strong with dedicated sponsors," maturing in 2031, and explained the strategy for managing it.
  • Hunsicker questioned the potential for share buybacks. Ohsberg said, "We take it under consideration all the time and taking into account other ways that we think that we need to deploy capital."
  • Ross Haberman, Rlh Investments: Asked about wealth management expansion. Handy described hiring business development officers and strategies for the nonprofit sector. Ohsberg confirmed "all in on average, it's about... 60 basis points" fee structure.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on margin guidance, expense control, and loan growth. Their questions reflected confidence in management's credit discipline but probed for more aggressive capital deployment and details on new initiatives.
  • Management's tone was confident and constructive, frequently using phrases like "we have high hopes and great expectations" and "we're very confident" regarding new hires and growth targets. During the Q&A, management provided detailed, transparent responses, especially around margin and credit quality, and showed a willingness to address analyst concerns directly.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed greater optimism, with less focus on credit losses and more on growth and profitability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from caution after elevated credit losses in Q3 to a more optimistic outlook in Q4, with explicit projections for margin expansion and loan growth.
  • Strategic focus moved from resolving credit exposures to expanding commercial banking and wealth management through new hires and targeted investments.
  • Key metrics improved: net income rose, net interest income and margin expanded, and noninterest income increased. Asset quality metrics showed improvement, with nonaccruals and past dues stabilizing.
  • Management expressed higher confidence, emphasizing "positive momentum" and "future growth." Analysts shifted from probing for explanations on credit losses and reserve adequacy in Q3 to exploring growth opportunities and capital deployment in Q4.
  • The tone of the discussion was more constructive and forward-looking, reflecting improved financial performance and optimism about new initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that reserve coverage remains on the lower side versus peers but expressed comfort with the current level and ongoing monitoring. The bank continues to monitor office loan exposures, with one classified loan described as "a long, slow nursing process" but currently performing and maturing in 2031.
  • Expense increases tied to hiring and branch expansion were flagged, but management provided clear guidance and rationale.
  • Analysts questioned the potential for early loan payoffs and the sustainability of current growth rates, as well as the possibility of renewed share buybacks, which management stated would be evaluated against other capital needs.

Final Takeaway

Washington Trust Bancorp closed 2025 with improved profitability, expanding net interest margin, and robust loan growth projections, underpinned by new hires and targeted investments in commercial and wealth management. Management expressed confidence in sustaining positive momentum through 2026, with disciplined expense control and ongoing attention to asset quality as the foundation for long-term value creation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Comcast shares rise after in-line Q4 report, overcoming tough environment
data.description:
Media and cable giant Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) reported a profit beat and an in-line revenue for the fourth quarter, overshadowing its wider-than-expected subscriber losses for broadband.

CMCSA stock is up more than 5% in late morning trading on the Nasdaq on Thursday.

In Q4, domestic broadband customer losses were 181,000, more than the Bloomberg consensus estimate of 168,194 losses, but domestic video customer losses were 245,000, lower than the 264,547 losses estimate, and domestic wireline additions went up by 364,000 compared to 307,000 additions last year.

Analysts at RBC Capital Markets said the report was in line with modest expectations, as the company enters a reset phase. Evercore ISI, in their first glance, said the report was largely as expected, including broadband. Wells Fargo said the 4Q'25 print was pretty in line with broadband subs and ARPU as expected in a tough operating environment.

Studio revenue fell 7.4% to $3.03B, hurt by lower theatrical revenue in Q4, which was due to tougher comparisons against prior-year releases, including Wicked and The Wild Robot, compared to current-quarter titles Wicked: For Good and Black Phone 2, the company said.

Revenue from its streaming service, Peacock, was $1.63B, up 23% from last year but below the estimate of $1.7B.

The ongoing momentum following the opening of Epic Universe in May last year helped boost revenue in the theme park business by 21.9% to $2.89B.

The total media segment revenue rose 5.5% in the quarter to $7.62B, helped in part by higher domestic advertising revenue, which included the positive impact from the launch of the NBA.

Revenue from connectivity and platforms, which brings in the bulk of total revenue, was down 1.1% at $20.24B.

Net income attributable to Comcast for the three months ended December 31 was $2.17B, or $0.60 per share, compared to $4.78B, or $1.24 per share, for the same period last year, weighed down by a nearly $2B rise in total costs and expenses.

On an adjusted per-share basis, the company earned $0.84, beating the average analyst estimate of $0.75 per share.

Revenue was up 1.2% to $32.31B but was below the $32.36B estimate.

data.title: Nasdaq dives -2% as big tech earnings fail to lift sentiments
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were lower on Thursday, as traders digested big tech company earnings that did not push indexes higher even as beats were reported.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.1% in midday trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -2.1%, and the Dow (DJI) was -0.4%.

Meta Platforms (META) gained more than 9% after posting its fourth-quarter earnings, where it beat Wall Street expectations for revenues and profitability. Shares were last +7.5%.

On the other hand, Tesla (TSLA) was -2.2% even as the EV giant reported top- and bottom-line beats, coupled with an upbeat outlook on Cybercab and robotaxi growth.

“We continue to believe Tesla could reach a $2 trillion market cap in early 2026 and $3 trillion in a bull case scenario by the end of 2026 as the golden AI chapter takes hold at Tesla,” said Dan Ives, senior equity research analyst at Wedbush Securities.

Microsoft (MSFT) was also down, -12.2%, even as the tech giant reported fiscal second-quarter results that topped estimates.

“For equities, the broadening continues, and so far not at the expense of the mega caps. This is good to see. The Mag 7 remain in their holding pattern while market breadth has become quite healthy,” said Jurrien Timmer, director of global macro at Fidelity.

On Wednesday, the Federal Reserve also kept its policy rate unchanged at 3.50%-3.75%

In other economic news, initial jobless claims declined to 209K, slightly above consensus, according to the Department of Labor. In addition, the U.S. international trade in goods and services deficit widened to $56.8B in November, a 94.6% increase.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) surged past $5,500 an ounce for the first time on Thursday, before paring some gains, now back around $5,200.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 1 basis point lower at 4.24%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was 2 basis points lower at 3.56%.
